source_paper_title: How to Design a Stable Serial Knockout Competition
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Lambers_2025, title={How to Design a Stable Serial Knockout Competition}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0352}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0352}, number={2}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Lambers, Roel and Pendavingh, Rudi and Spieksma, Frits}, year={2025}, month=may, pages={1421–1432} }
context:
Page 13, end of Section 5.2 (after Theorem 3) and reiterated in Section 7 (Discussion), page 14.

For \(n = 2^k > 8\) players, we do not know whether there exist stable SKCs that are inequivalent to the ones that we construct in this paper, not even in the case of \(n=16\) players.

We have also shown that the construction to create stable SKCs generates a unique (up to permutation of the players) tournament for eight players; it remains open to generalize this for larger powers of two.
title: Classify stable serial knockout competitions up to equivalence for larger powers of two
background_latex:
Fix an integer \(k\ge 2\) and let \(n=2^k\). A (balanced) single-elimination knockout tournament on a player set \(P\) with \(|P|=n\) is a rooted binary tree with \(n\) leaves labeled bijectively by the players; the tournament has \(k\) rounds, and players \(x,x'\in P\) are said to be able to meet in round \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\) if, given the fixed bracket, there is a possible sequence of earlier match outcomes under which they face each other in round \(i\). Formally, for a knockout tournament \(T\) on \(P\), define \(v_T(x,x')=i\) if \(x\) and \(x'\) can meet in round \(i\) in \(T\).

A serial knockout competition (SKC) on \(P\) is a set \(\mathcal{T}\) of \(n-1\) knockout tournaments on the same player set \(P\). For each round \(i\), the SKC \(\mathcal{T}\) is called stable in round \(i\) if there exists a constant \(c_i\) such that for every distinct pair \(x\ne x'\) in \(P\),
\[
\#\{T\in\mathcal{T}: v_T(x,x')=i\}=c_i.
\]
It is stable if it is stable in all rounds \(i=1,\dots,k\). For an SKC with \(|\mathcal{T}|=n-1\), counting implies \(c_i=2^{i-1}\).

Two knockout tournaments \(T,T'\) on player sets \(P,P'\) are considered identical if \(v_T(p,q)=v_{T'}(p,q)\) for all pairs \(p,q\) in the common player set; and two SKCs \(\mathcal{T}\) on \(P\) and \(\mathcal{T}'\) on \(P'\) are called equivalent if there exists a bijection \(s:P\to P'\) such that \(\mathcal{T}'=\{T(s): T\in \mathcal{T}\}\), where \(T(s)\) is obtained from \(T\) by relabeling each player \(p\in P\) as \(s(p)\). The paper constructs, for every \(n=2^k\), a stable SKC via algebraic structure on \(\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\).
problem_definition_latex: For \(n=2^k>8\), determine whether every stable serial knockout competition \(\mathcal{T}\) on \(n\) players is equivalent (via relabeling of players) to the algebraic stable SKC constructed using \(\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\); equivalently, decide whether there exist stable SKCs on \(n\) players that are inequivalent to that construction (in particular, for \(n=16\)).
latex:
Fix an integer \(k\ge 2\) and let \(n=2^k\). A (balanced) single-elimination knockout tournament on a player set \(P\) with \(|P|=n\) is a rooted binary tree with \(n\) leaves labeled bijectively by the players; the tournament has \(k\) rounds, and players \(x,x'\in P\) are said to be able to meet in round \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\) if, given the fixed bracket, there is a possible sequence of earlier match outcomes under which they face each other in round \(i\). Formally, for a knockout tournament \(T\) on \(P\), define \(v_T(x,x')=i\) if \(x\) and \(x'\) can meet in round \(i\) in \(T\).

A serial knockout competition (SKC) on \(P\) is a set \(\mathcal{T}\) of \(n-1\) knockout tournaments on the same player set \(P\). For each round \(i\), the SKC \(\mathcal{T}\) is called stable in round \(i\) if there exists a constant \(c_i\) such that for every distinct pair \(x\ne x'\) in \(P\),
\[
\#\{T\in\mathcal{T}: v_T(x,x')=i\}=c_i.
\]
It is stable if it is stable in all rounds \(i=1,\dots,k\). For an SKC with \(|\mathcal{T}|=n-1\), counting implies \(c_i=2^{i-1}\).

Two knockout tournaments \(T,T'\) on player sets \(P,P'\) are considered identical if \(v_T(p,q)=v_{T'}(p,q)\) for all pairs \(p,q\) in the common player set; and two SKCs \(\mathcal{T}\) on \(P\) and \(\mathcal{T}'\) on \(P'\) are called equivalent if there exists a bijection \(s:P\to P'\) such that \(\mathcal{T}'=\{T(s): T\in \mathcal{T}\}\), where \(T(s)\) is obtained from \(T\) by relabeling each player \(p\in P\) as \(s(p)\). The paper constructs, for every \(n=2^k\), a stable SKC via algebraic structure on \(\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\).

For \(n=2^k>8\), determine whether every stable serial knockout competition \(\mathcal{T}\) on \(n\) players is equivalent (via relabeling of players) to the algebraic stable SKC constructed using \(\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\); equivalently, decide whether there exist stable SKCs on \(n\) players that are inequivalent to that construction (in particular, for \(n=16\)).
location_in_paper: Page 13, end of Section 5.2 (after Theorem 3) and reiterated in Section 7 (Discussion), page 14.
open_problem_quote:
For \(n = 2^k > 8\) players, we do not know whether there exist stable SKCs that are inequivalent to the ones that we construct in this paper, not even in the case of \(n=16\) players.

We have also shown that the construction to create stable SKCs generates a unique (up to permutation of the players) tournament for eight players; it remains open to generalize this for larger powers of two.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: For a fixed small size such as \(n=16\), one can search over candidate SKCs (sets of \(n-1\) brackets) using SAT/ILP with constraints enforcing stability counts \(c_i=2^{i-1}\), and then test equivalence to the \(\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\) construction via canonical labeling/invariants. Finding a single feasible SKC inequivalent to the constructed one would resolve the existential direction for that \(n\).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate SKCs can be encoded as \(n-1\) permutations (seedings) or as bracket partitions, and a fitness signal can be computed from deviations of pair-counts from the target \(2^{i-1}\) across rounds, plus a penalty for being equivalent to known constructions. However, equivalence testing and the combinatorial search space grow rapidly with \(n\), so iterative search may be costly beyond \(n=16\) or \(32\) without strong symmetry-breaking.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The defining constraint for a stable SKC on \(n=2^k\) players is that for each round \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\) and each unordered pair \(\{x,x'\}\), the number of brackets \(T\) in the family \(\mathcal T\) with \(v_T(x,x')=i\) is constant (necessarily \(c_i=2^{i-1}\) when \(|\mathcal T|=n-1\)). Lambers–Pendavingh–Spieksma construct such families for all \(2^k\) using algebra over \(\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\), and they prove uniqueness up to relabeling only in the smallest nontrivial case \(n=8\) (via the Fano plane). The open problem asks whether this algebraic construction is essentially the only one for \(n>8\), e.g. \(n=16\), or whether inequivalent stable SKCs exist.

Among the forward citations provided, the only citing work is an operations-research survey that briefly summarizes the existence result and its Galois-field connection but does not contribute new structural, classification, or nonisomorphism results for stable SKCs. Consequently, based on the available forward-citation evidence, the classification/uniqueness question for \(n=2^k>8\) remains open. Promising directions suggested by the original paper’s viewpoint include translating stability into incidence/association-scheme language on pairs of players (where \(v_T\) induces a hierarchy of partitions by “round of possible meeting”), analyzing automorphism groups of the \(\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\)-constructed SKC, and searching for alternative constructions via difference sets, orthogonal arrays, or other finite-geometry designs that could yield inequivalent SKCs for \(n=16\) or prove rigidity.

sources.title: Tournament design: A review from an operational research perspective
sources.theorem_or_result: This paper is a survey/review and does not prove new classification theorems about stable serial knockout competitions. Its only direct statement relevant to the open problem is an expository summary of Lambers et al. (2024): stable serial knockout competitions (serial SKO with \,n-1\, subtournaments) can be constructed for \,n=2^k\, so that each pair of players can meet equally often in each round, using a connection to Galois fields.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for a classification/uniqueness result: whether every stable SKC on \,n=2^k>8\, is equivalent to the \,\mathrm{GF}(2^k)\,-based construction (e.g. for \,n=16\,). This citing paper does not address uniqueness or inequivalence, does not analyze automorphisms/equivalence classes of stable SKCs, and does not provide new constructions beyond citing Lambers et al. (2024). It therefore provides background context and a pointer to the original construction but no progress on classification.
